schools.summary: Gill: after the death of Deborah and Barak, during whose life they kept to the pure worship of God.

themes: the children of Israel did evil in the sight of the LORD: and the LORD delivered them into the hand of Midian seven years
the swift relapse when faithfulness leans on leaders not the heart
Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua, and... the elders (Josh. 24:31)

schools.summary: Gill: Midian was too strong for them, no wonder when the Lord delivered them into their hand; Israel made dens and caves in the mountains.

themes: the children of Israel made them the dens which are in the mountains, and caves, and strong holds
sin bringing God's people low, driving them to wretched refuges
they wandered... in dens and caves of the earth (Heb. 11:38)

schools.summary: Gill: the Midianites gave no disturbance in winter and seedtime, but came when the harvest was near ripe; the Amalekites and children of the east came with them.

themes: when Israel had sown, that the Midianites came up, and the Amalekites, and the children of the east
sin giving God's people to enemies who devour their increase
thou shalt sow, but thou shalt not reap (Mic. 6:15)

schools.summary: Gill: they formed a camp and sent out parties to plunder, fixing their tents among them; left no sustenance, neither sheep, nor ox, nor ass.

themes: they... destroyed the increase of the earth... and left no sustenance for Israel, neither sheep, nor ox, nor ass
the forsaking of God stripping his people of all earthly good
that which the palmerworm hath left hath the locust eaten (Joel 1:4)

schools.summary: Gill: they brought their flocks and herds to eat the increase; came as grasshoppers for multitude, with camels without number.

themes: they came as grasshoppers for multitude... and they entered into the land to destroy it
God's people overrun by enemies past number when he withdraws
a nation is come up upon my land, strong, and without number (Joel 1:6)

schools.summary: Gill: they were reduced very low, brought into famishing circumstances year after year.
Ellicott: the LORD bringing them low.

themes: Israel was greatly impoverished because of the Midianites; and the children of Israel cried unto the LORD
affliction the means God uses to bring his people back to him
in their affliction they will seek me early (Hos. 5:15)

schools.summary: Gill: they cried because of their oppressions and ill usage, not because of their sins, of which they seemed not yet sensible.

themes: when the children of Israel cried unto the LORD because of the Midianites
the cry of distress not yet the cry of true repentance
godly sorrow worketh repentance... but the sorrow of the world worketh death (2 Cor. 7:10)

schools.summary: Gill: it was 'a man, a prophet,' not an angel, sent to them; he reminded them, I brought you up from Egypt.

themes: the LORD sent a prophet... I brought you up from Egypt, and brought you forth out of the house of bondage
God preparing his people for deliverance by a word of conviction
I am the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the house of bondage (Exod. 20:2)

schools.summary: Gill: God delivered them even after Egypt, when the Egyptians pursued and overtook them at the Red sea; drave out their oppressors and gave them their land.

themes: I delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians, and out of the hand of all that oppressed you... and gave you their land
the remembrance of God's many mercies binding his people to him
they remembered not the multitude of thy mercies (Ps. 106:7)

schools.summary: Gill: he was the covenant God of them and their fathers, whom they ought not to have forsaken.
Ellicott: the gods of the Amorites they were not to fear.

themes: I am the LORD your God; fear not the gods of the Amorites... but ye have not obeyed my voice
the people bound to God alone, their misery rooted in disobedience
they obeyed not... but walked in the counsels... of their evil heart (Jer. 7:24)

schools.summary: Gill: this was not a created angel but the Angel of Jehovah's presence, the Word and Son of God, expressly called Jehovah.

themes: there came an angel of the LORD, and sat under an oak... his son Gideon threshed wheat by the winepress, to hide it from the Midianites
the Angel of the LORD, the Son of God, meeting the lowly at his labour
I am found of them that sought me not (Isa. 65:1)

schools.summary: Gill: the angel stood before him in his sight, Gideon being busy threshing; he said, The LORD is with thee, thou mighty man of valour.

themes: The LORD is with thee, thou mighty man of valour
God naming his servant by what his grace will make him
as a prince hast thou power with God and with men, and hast prevailed (Gen. 32:28)

schools.summary: Gill: Gideon took him not for an angel, but for some illustrious person.
Keil & Delitzsch: his question, why is all this befallen us, and where are all his miracles?

themes: if the LORD be with us, why then is all this befallen us? and where be all his miracles which our fathers told us of?
the honest perplexity of faith under affliction
why art thou cast down, O my soul?... hope thou in God (Ps. 42:11)

schools.summary: Gill: the same called the angel of the Lord, who was no other than Jehovah himself; he said, Go in this thy might; have not I sent thee?

themes: Go in this thy might, and thou shalt save Israel from the hand of the Midianites: have not I sent thee?
the strength of God's servants in his commission and sending
I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me (Phil. 4:13)

schools.summary: Gill: Gideon began to think he was some extraordinary person sent of God, and pleads his unfitness; his family poor in Manasseh, he the least.

themes: wherewith shall I save Israel? behold, my family is poor in Manasseh, and I am the least in my father's house
God choosing the lowly and least for his greatest works
God hath chosen the weak things... and base things... and things which are despised (1 Cor. 1:27-28)

schools.summary: Gill: the Targum reads, 'my Word shall be thy help,' sufficient to answer all objections from his meanness and weakness.

themes: Surely I will be with thee, and thou shalt smite the Midianites as one man
the presence of God the answer to every fear and weakness
certainly I will be with thee (Exod. 3:12)

schools.summary: Gill: he asks this seeing he had found grace, by the salutation, the commission, and the promise.
Keil & Delitzsch: him asking for a sign.

themes: If now I have found grace in thy sight, then shew me a sign that thou talkest with me
God condescending to confirm the weak faith of his servants
Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief (Mark 9:24)

schools.summary: Gill: Gideon intended to go to his father's house to fetch food to entertain him.
Keil & Delitzsch: the request, Depart not hence till I bring my present.

themes: Depart not hence... until I... bring forth my present, and set it before thee. And he said, I will tarry
the reverence that honours God's presence with gifts
bring an offering, and come before him (1 Chr. 16:29)

schools.summary: Gill: Gideon went into his own or his father's house to prepare it.
Clarke: the manner of Arab hospitality, the flesh in a basket and the broth in a pot.

themes: Gideon... made ready a kid, and unleavened cakes... and brought it out unto him under the oak, and presented it
the reverence that gives God its best though it cost much
I will not offer... unto the LORD my God of that which doth cost me nothing (2 Sam. 24:24)

schools.summary: Gill: instead of sitting to eat, he bade him do this.
Clarke: the angel intended to make the flesh and bread an offering.

themes: Take the flesh and the unleavened cakes, and lay them upon this rock, and pour out the broth
the meal turned to sacrifice, the visitant divine and worthy of worship
they drank of that spiritual Rock... and that Rock was Christ (1 Cor. 10:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the staff with which he walked, appearing as a traveller.
Keil & Delitzsch: the fire rising out of the rock and the angel departing.

themes: there rose up fire out of the rock, and consumed the flesh and the unleavened cakes... and the angel of the LORD departed
God witnessing his acceptance of true offering by fire
the LORD... answered him from heaven by fire upon the altar (1 Chr. 21:26)

schools.summary: Gill: he perceived it by the miracle wrought and the manner of his departure.
Clarke: the elliptical cry, a natural expression of distress, fearing he should die.

themes: Alas, O Lord GOD! for because I have seen an angel of the LORD face to face
the holiness of God striking fear into sinful man
I have seen God face to face, and my life is preserved (Gen. 32:30)

schools.summary: Gill: this was by a secret impulse, or a voice from heaven; the angel after ascending causing the voice to be heard; Peace be unto thee; fear not.

themes: And the LORD said unto him, Peace be unto thee; fear not: thou shalt not die
the LORD speaking peace to those who fear before him
Peace be unto you (John 20:19); let not your heart be troubled (John 14:27)

schools.summary: Gill: he built it on the rock where his provisions had been consumed by fire, as a token of divine acceptance; called it Jehovah-shalom.

themes: Then Gideon built an altar there unto the LORD, and called it Jehovahshalom
the LORD himself the peace of his people, a name to be remembered
he is our peace (Eph. 2:14); having made peace through the blood of his cross (Col. 1:20)

schools.summary: Gill: this was the night following the day the angel appeared.
Keil & Delitzsch: the command to throw down the altar of Baal.

themes: throw down the altar of Baal that thy father hath, and cut down the grove that is by it
reformation beginning at home, the idols of our own house first cast down
cleanse first that which is within (Matt. 23:26)

schools.summary: Gill: the altar on the rock where the provisions were consumed by fire, near where Jehovah-shalom had been built; offer with the wood of the grove.

themes: build an altar unto the LORD thy God upon the top of this rock... and offer a burnt sacrifice with the wood of the grove
the removal of idolatry followed by the setting up of true worship
turn from these vanities unto the living God (Acts 14:15)

schools.summary: Gill: he took ten men he could confide in, like himself not having bowed to Baal; he did it by night for fear.

themes: did as the LORD had said unto him... because he feared his father's household... he did it by night
God accepting obedience mingled with weakness and fear
a bruised reed shall he not break (Matt. 12:20)

schools.summary: Gill: they came to pay their morning devotions to Baal.
Keil & Delitzsch: the altar cast down and the bullock offered.

themes: the altar of Baal was cast down, and the grove was cut down... and the second bullock was offered upon the altar that was built
the open overthrow of the idol a public testimony that the LORD alone is God
the idols he shall utterly abolish (Isa. 2:18)

schools.summary: Gill: they were struck with amazement, unable to devise who could be so daring.
Keil & Delitzsch: them asking, Who hath done this thing?

themes: Who hath done this thing?... Gideon the son of Joash hath done this thing
those who stand against idolatry found out and opposed
all that will live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution (2 Tim. 3:12)

schools.summary: Gill: the principal inhabitants went in a body to Joash, the chief magistrate, to have justice done.
Clarke: them demanding his death.

themes: Bring out thy son, that he may die: because he hath cast down the altar of Baal
blinded men defending their idols and persecuting the servant of God
whosoever killeth you will think that he doeth God service (John 16:2)

schools.summary: Gill: Joash said this to the accusers who required his son to be given up to death.
Keil & Delitzsch: if Baal be a god, let him plead for himself.

themes: if he be a god, let him plead for himself, because one hath cast down his altar
the impotence of the idol exposed, the vanity of false gods
they have mouths, but they speak not... they that make them are like unto them (Ps. 115:5-8)

schools.summary: Gill: Joash called his son Gideon by that name, 'Let Baal contend,' because he had thrown down Baal's altar.

themes: he called him Jerubbaal, saying, Let Baal plead against him, because he hath thrown down his altar
a new name a standing testimony against idolatry
the LORD he is God; there is none else beside him (Deut. 4:35)

schools.summary: Gill: it was their usual time of gathering to spoil Israel, not in revenge for the altar; they pitched in the valley of Jezreel.

themes: all the Midianites and the Amalekites and the children of the east were gathered together... and pitched in the valley of Jezreel
God suffering his enemies to gather in full strength that his deliverance be glorious
associate yourselves... and ye shall be broken... for God is with us (Isa. 8:9-10)

schools.summary: Gill: not the spirit of prophecy, but a spirit of fortitude and courage; the Hebrew is 'clothed' Gideon.

themes: the Spirit of the LORD came upon Gideon, and he blew a trumpet; and Abiezer was gathered after him
the servant of God clothed with power by the Spirit for his work
tarry ye... until ye be endued with power from on high (Luke 24:49)

schools.summary: Gill: he called the Abiezrites by the trumpet, and sent messengers to the rest of Manasseh and the other tribes; they came up.

themes: he sent messengers throughout all Manasseh... and unto Asher, and unto Zebulun, and unto Naphtali; and they came up
God drawing his people together at the call of the leader he sends
they came to the help of the LORD (Judg. 5:23)

schools.summary: Gill: he said this to God in prayer.
Clarke: Gideon was very bold, yet God bore with his weakness.

themes: And Gideon said unto God, If thou wilt save Israel by mine hand, as thou hast said
the weakness of faith craving assurance even after God has spoken
if we believe not, yet he abideth faithful (2 Tim. 2:13)

schools.summary: Gill: the fleece on the threshing floor, exposed to the open air, where the dew might fall.
Keil & Delitzsch: the sign of the fleece.

themes: if the dew be on the fleece only, and it be dry upon all the earth beside, then shall I know that thou wilt save Israel
God condescending to confirm faith; the dew a figure of his grace
I will be as the dew unto Israel (Hos. 14:5)

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord condescended to work this miracle for the confirmation of his faith, the fleece wet and the ground dry.
Ellicott: the dew wrung out, a bowl full.

themes: he... wringed the dew out of the fleece, a bowl full of water
God graciously strengthening weak faith with a full token
according to your faith be it unto you (Matt. 9:29)

schools.summary: Gill: he asked in the same way, on the morning after the first miracle.
Keil & Delitzsch: him saying, Let not thine anger be hot against me.

themes: Let not thine anger be hot against me, and I will speak but this once: let me prove... but this once with the fleece
seeking assurance with humility and the fear of presuming on God's patience
let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace (Heb. 4:16)

schools.summary: Gill: the night being the season in which the dew falls.
Keil & Delitzsch: dry upon the fleece only, and dew on all the ground.

themes: God did so that night: for it was dry upon the fleece only, and there was dew on all the ground
God's patient grace settling faith; the dew at last on all the ground
the earth shall be full of the knowledge of the LORD (Isa. 11:9)
